topics.name: Anxiety
topics.description: The experience of worry, fear, or dread about circumstances beyond one's control. Pastorally distinct from clinical anxiety disorder — these passages address the soul's tendency toward anxious striving and invite trust in God's provision and presence.
intent_examples: I'm overwhelmed with fear about the future
how do I stop being anxious
I feel paralyzed by what might happen

passages.rationale: Paul's direct pastoral instruction to bring anxious thoughts to God in prayer, with the promise of a peace that transcends understanding as a guard over heart and mind.
passages.context_note: Written from prison — Paul's context of genuine suffering gives the command weight; this is not shallow positivity but hard-won counsel.

passages.rationale: Jesus addresses worry about provision (food, clothing, future) directly, grounding relief in the character of the Father and the priority of the Kingdom.
passages.context_note: Part of the Sermon on the Mount; 'do not worry' is a command, not a suggestion — but Jesus pairs it with a theological reason, not mere willpower.

passages.rationale: Casts anxiety as something that can be actively given over to God, grounded in the affirmation that God cares personally for the individual.
passages.context_note: The Greek word for 'cast' (epiripsantes) implies a deliberate, forceful act — not passive resignation but an act of entrusting.

passages.rationale: Describes God as a present refuge even when the world feels like it is falling apart — meets anxiety about catastrophic circumstances with imagery of divine stability.
passages.context_note: The psalm acknowledges real upheaval ('though the earth gives way') before asserting confidence — it does not minimize the threat.

caution_flags: Distinguish soul-care from clinical treatment — for persistent anxiety disorders, Scripture is a companion to professional care, not a replacement.
topics.editorial_notes: Philippians 4:6-7 and Matthew 6:25-34 are the two anchors; the supporting passages extend the theme of active trust rather than merely repeating the same note.

topics.name: Grief
topics.description: Sorrow arising from loss — death of a loved one, broken relationships, shattered expectations, or cumulative disappointment. Scripture validates lament as a faithful response and points toward the God who enters into grief rather than standing apart from it.
intent_examples: I can't stop crying and I don't know why
how do I deal with death
nothing will ever be the same again

passages.rationale: Jesus weeps at the tomb of Lazarus even knowing he will raise him — the shortest verse in Scripture ('Jesus wept') carries enormous pastoral weight as evidence that God is not unmoved by human grief.
passages.context_note: Read in context: Mary's words in v.32 ('if you had been here') and the crowd's grief (v.33) show Jesus entering into real mourning, not performing it. He weeps with those who weep.

passages.rationale: A direct promise of God's nearness to those who are brokenhearted and crushed in spirit — meets grief with assurance of divine presence, not an explanation or a fix.
passages.context_note: Psalm 34 is an acrostic of praise, making this verse's tenderness more striking — grief and praise coexist in the same poem.

passages.rationale: Models honest lament — the psalmist cries out 'My God, why have you forsaken me?' — giving voice to the experience of divine absence without abandoning faith. Particularly valuable when grief feels like spiritual abandonment.
passages.context_note: Jesus quotes this psalm from the cross (Matt 27:46), canonically linking human grief to the suffering of Christ.

passages.rationale: Grounds the grieving person in the unbreakable love of God — no loss, no death, no depth of sorrow can sever that bond. Offers eschatological comfort without minimizing present pain.
passages.context_note: Best offered after space has been given for lament; can feel dismissive if introduced too quickly.

caution_flags: Avoid passages that could be read as minimizing grief (e.g., 'all things work together for good' in isolation); pair with lament passages first.
Be attentive to whether grief is complicated by guilt or estrangement — the pastoral need may differ significantly.
topics.editorial_notes: John 11:35 is best referenced with its surrounding context (vv.32-36) to preserve the full picture of Jesus entering into grief. The lament psalms are essential companions here.

topics.name: Doubt
topics.description: Intellectual or existential uncertainty about God, faith, or Scripture — ranging from honest questions to a crisis of belief. Scripture treats doubt as a recurring experience of genuine faith rather than its opposite, and several key narratives model honest struggle leading to deeper trust.
intent_examples: I'm not sure I believe anymore
how do I have faith when I can't see God
I have so many questions about Christianity
I feel like God isn't real
what if I'm wrong about all of this

passages.rationale: Thomas's refusal to believe without evidence, and Jesus's response — not rebuke but invitation to touch and see — models that Jesus meets doubt with presence and evidence rather than condemnation.
passages.context_note: The final beatitude ('blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed', v.29) should not be read as a rebuke of Thomas but as an encouragement for future believers; Jesus still appeared to Thomas.

passages.rationale: 'I believe; help my unbelief!' — one of the most honest prayers in Scripture, spoken by a father asking for his son's healing. Validates mixed faith (belief and doubt coexisting) as sufficient to bring to Jesus.
passages.context_note: Jesus heals the child in response to this cry — doubt did not disqualify the prayer. Powerful for those who feel their faith is too weak or impure.

passages.rationale: Asaph's extended honest wrestling with why the wicked prosper — models sustained intellectual and emotional doubt within the psalter, resolved not by argument but by entering the sanctuary and gaining God's perspective.
passages.context_note: The resolution (vv.17ff) comes through worship and encounter, not answered questions — important for doubters who are looking for intellectual resolution alone.

passages.rationale: Defines faith as 'confidence in what we hope for, assurance about what we do not see' — acknowledges that faith always involves unseen realities, so doubt is not its absence but its context.
passages.context_note: Use carefully — can sound like intellectual bypass if offered without engaging the substance of the doubt.

caution_flags: Distinguish intellectual doubt (apologetics may help) from existential doubt rooted in suffering or loss (pastoral care is the primary need).
Avoid implying that doubt is sinful; the biblical narrative consistently shows God engaging doubters directly.
topics.editorial_notes: Mark 9:24 is often the single most resonant verse for someone experiencing doubt — the raw honesty of the prayer tends to break through defenses. John 20 and Psalm 73 provide the narrative and wisdom-literature counterparts.
